## II. How to Read the Map

::: {.aside .meta-note}
**META:** Operational, not conceptual. The intro already made the empirical-vs-axes point; this section does not re-argue it. It lays out the machinery: the order of operations, the three framing choices, the tree, and a pointer to the cruxes. No "spine," no "carving joints." Emphasis stays sparing.
:::

Sorting a disagreement into those two kinds is the move the rest of this relies on, so it is worth stating the order of operations plainly. When an argument stalls, first check whether you and the other person share a frame: the same definition of doom, the same horizon, the same standard for what counts as evidence. If you don't, that is the disagreement, and trading studies is wasted effort, because the numbers were never measuring the same thing. Only once the frame is shared is it worth finding the crux, the factual bet you actually differ on, because that is the part an argument can move.

Three framing choices do most of the damage when they go unstated, and you met two of them in the intro: what counts as doom, from extinction to permanent loss of control to the harms already arriving, and over what horizon, the next few years or the next century. The third is how much a model's current behavior should weigh against a structural prior. These are the labels you set on the [interactive tool](https://lifeuniversesafety.com/doom-assumptions/index.html) before any number means anything, and two people who set them differently can trade p(doom) figures all day without contradicting each other once.

Everything else hangs on the tree from the first two posts: does AI raise the danger at all, through one dominant system or many, does that system model what it is doing, does it expect the catastrophe it causes. The gallery in §III walks the tree and puts a worldview at each branch. The cruxes, the bets where people actually move, come after the gallery in §IV, where they read more clearly against the positions they separate.
